content:
Justice is interpreted in the courts, but its foundation is laid here.

Today marked a significant milestone, my first visit to the Indian Parliament, made possible through the privilege extended by Shri S.K. Patri Sir and Nigamananda Prusty.

Walking through both the historic and the new corridors of power offered a profound lesson in how national identity and policy intersect:

The Architecture of Governance: 
Moving from the historic chambers to the advanced new building, the integration of Indian symbolism is striking. The Lok Sabha, designed around a peacock theme, stretches out under a sea of vibrant green carpets, while the Rajya Sabha mirrors the prestige of the lotus theme.

The Art of Policy: 
A massive bronze mural of Samudra Manthan lines the wall, serving as a powerful visual metaphor for the churning, debate, and effort that goes into framing national policies.
Time and Constitution: At the center hangs the Foucault pendulum, charting time alongside the solar positions of the star constellations to freeze-frame the exact moment our Constitution was incorporated.

As someone in the legal field, the highlight was standing in the exact spaces where the Federal Court of India and later the Supreme Court, functioned before its dedicated building was constructed in the late 1950s. Seeing the emblems of the major princely states still preserved there connects you directly to the roots of our judicial history.
An unforgettable experience that bridges the history of Indian law with the future of its governance. 🏛️⚖️

#ParliamentOfIndia #LegalHistory #Jurisprudence #IndianConstitution #PublicPolicy
